--- title: "Data transformation: Rows, *Advanced*" author: "Nicky Wakim" title-slide-attributes: data-background-color: "#3070BF" format: revealjs: theme: "../simple_NW.scss" chalkboard: true slide-number: true show-slide-number: all width: 1955 height: 1100 footer: "Data transformation: Rows, *Advanced*" html-math-method: mathjax highlight-style: ayu code-annotations: hover execute: echo: true freeze: auto --- ::: {.cell} ::: # ## Filtering requires a little logic - We can chain multiple criteria using the [`&` (AND)]{style="color:darkorange"} or [`|` (OR) operators]{style="color:darkorange"}. - But we need to review a little logic before we do this. - If we wanted to return participants who identified as - `Female` **and** are in 10th grade, - we would use an `&` to chain these criteria together: ``` sex == "Female" & grade == "10th" ``` - Note that *SPELLING AND CASE MATTER*! - You must write the name of the column exactly and - also the value (i.e. "Female" not "female") exactly. ## Filtering with `&` (AND) criteria ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} hrs_data |> filter(diab == "Yes" & degree == "Master's degree") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 81 × 31 hhid pn birthyr birthmo birthdate ed degree proxy coupled female 1 557411 010 1970 3 3698 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 2 555572 020 1966 9 2449 17 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 3 555658 010 1953 3 -2511 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 4 552554 020 1974 5 5187 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 5 557791 010 1971 1 4032 16 Master's d… Resp… No spo… Female 6 550718 010 1951 1 -3214 17 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Male 7 559963 010 1969 3 3361 16 Master's d… Resp… No spo… Male 8 550401 010 1969 12 3636 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Male 9 551771 010 1958 9 -443 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 10 551334 010 1959 10 -47 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female # ℹ 71 more rows # ℹ 21 more variables: age_months , age , race_original , # smokeever , smoke2now , drink , weight , # self_health , high_bp , diab , cancer , lung_dis , # heart_dis , strk , psych_prob , sleep_prob , # arthritis , cond_count , r16hosp , r16cesd , # income ``` ::: ::: - Note that we could also use the comma in this case: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} hrs_data |> filter(diab == "Yes", # <1> Comma is equivalent to "&" within filter() degree == "Master's degree") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 81 × 31 hhid pn birthyr birthmo birthdate ed degree proxy coupled female 1 557411 010 1970 3 3698 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 2 555572 020 1966 9 2449 17 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 3 555658 010 1953 3 -2511 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 4 552554 020 1974 5 5187 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 5 557791 010 1971 1 4032 16 Master's d… Resp… No spo… Female 6 550718 010 1951 1 -3214 17 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Male 7 559963 010 1969 3 3361 16 Master's d… Resp… No spo… Male 8 550401 010 1969 12 3636 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Male 9 551771 010 1958 9 -443 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 10 551334 010 1959 10 -47 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female # ℹ 71 more rows # ℹ 21 more variables: age_months , age , race_original , # smokeever , smoke2now , drink , weight , # self_health , high_bp , diab , cancer , lung_dis , # heart_dis , strk , psych_prob , sleep_prob , # arthritis , cond_count , r16hosp , r16cesd , # income ``` ::: ::: ## Filtering with `|` (OR) criteria If we wanted patients who were `Female` [**or**]{style="color:darkorange"} in 10th grade we would use a `|` to chain these criteria together. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} hrs_data |> filter(diab == "Yes" | degree == "Master's degree") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1,133 × 31 hhid pn birthyr birthmo birthdate ed degree proxy coupled female 1 557411 010 1970 3 3698 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Female 2 551228 010 1966 10 2479 12 None Resp… No spo… Male 3 557020 020 1969 2 3302 16 Master's d… Resp… Marrie… Male 4 553327 010 1970 12 4001 12 High schoo… Resp… No spo… Female 5 550524 010 1952 12 -2573 16 Master's d… Resp… No spo… Male 6 557080 010 1966 11 2510 NA Resp… No spo… Female 7 559263 010 1941 3 -6652 8 None Resp… Marrie… Female 8 556907 010 1948 8 -4522 16 Profession… Resp… Marrie… Male 9 550299 020 1973 1 4822 NA Resp… Marrie… Female 10 552423 020 1966 3 2265 13 Associate'… Resp… Marrie… Female # ℹ 1,123 more rows # ℹ 21 more variables: age_months , age , race_original , # smokeever , smoke2now , drink , weight , # self_health , high_bp , diab , cancer , lung_dis , # heart_dis , strk , psych_prob , sleep_prob , # arthritis , cond_count , r16hosp , r16cesd , # income ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-warning icon="false"} ## Think about it: Which of the AND vs. OR code blocks will return a larger number of participants? ::: ## More filter examples - See [this slide](https://jminnier-berd-r-courses.netlify.app/02-data-wrangling-tidyverse/02_data_wrangling_slides_part1.html#27){target="_blank"} from a previous BERD workshop. - Also read the [filter section](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-transform.html#rows){target="_blank"} in the "Data Transformation" chapter from *R for Data Science (2e)*.
### More about comparison and logical operators Click [here](https://www.datamentor.io/r-programming/operator/){target="_blank"} for a useful reference for all the different operators (both logical and comparison) that you can use. ## Using %in% # `arrange()` ## `arrange()` after `count()`-ing categorical data - Another example of when `arrange()` is useful ::: columns ::: {.column width="48%"} - We can also use `count()` to count all of the unique values for a categorical variable. - This will also show `NA` category, which is the special missing variable type: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} hrs_data |> count(age) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 65 × 2 age n 1 31 4 2 34 3 3 35 5 4 36 2 5 37 2 6 38 2 7 39 3 8 40 4 9 41 10 10 42 12 # ℹ 55 more rows ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="4%"} ::: ::: {.column width="48%"} - Sometimes it helps to arrange by that category if you have many categories to view. - This can also alert us if there are categorical coding mistakes (such as misspellings) in our data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} hrs_data |> count(age) |> arrange(n) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 65 × 2 age n 1 86 1 2 92 1 3 36 2 4 37 2 5 38 2 6 85 2 7 89 2 8 90 2 9 93 2 10 94 2 # ℹ 55 more rows ``` ::: ::: ::: :::
